confidence.disclosure: Muawiya, the first Umayyad caliph, is well documented by year in early Arabic chronicles, though specific months and days are usually not preserved. The major events of his life - his conversion at the conquest of Mecca, his appointment as governor of Syria by Umar, the first civil war, the arbitration after Siffin, and his caliphate from 661 to 680 - are firmly placed by year. The journey is geographically interesting: Mecca, Medina, the Syrian frontier, and the new capital at Damascus.

journey.event: Born in Mecca to Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, the leader of the Umayyad clan and one of the most powerful men of Quraysh, and Hind bint Utba. Grows up in the leading family of Mecca during the years before Islam.

When the Battle of Badr is fought in 624, his older brother Hanzala is killed fighting against the Muslims. The family of Abu Sufyan becomes one of the leaders of Qurashi resistance to Muhammad in the following years.

When the Prophet Muhammad conquers Mecca in 630 (8 AH) at the head of 10,000 men, Abu Sufyan and his family submit and accept Islam. Mu'awiya becomes one of the new Muslims (al-tulaqa') and is later employed by the Prophet as a scribe of revelation.

Joins the Muslim armies sent by the caliph Abu Bakr to conquer Syria, fighting alongside his older brother Yazid ibn Abi Sufyan, who is one of the four commanders. Participates in the early conquest of southern Syria and Palestine.

When his brother Yazid dies of plague in 639, the caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab appoints Mu'awiya governor of Damascus. Over the following decade Umar consolidates his governorship to include all of greater Syria.

After Umar's assassination, the new caliph Uthman (a fellow Umayyad) confirms Mu'awiya as governor of Syria and gives him a free hand. Mu'awiya builds the first Muslim navy and launches raids on Cyprus (649) and against Byzantine fleets in the eastern Mediterranean.

When Uthman is murdered by rebels in Medina in June 656, Mu'awiya refuses to recognise the new caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib, demanding first that the killers of Uthman (his Umayyad kinsman) be punished. He raises the bloodied shirt of Uthman in the mosque of Damascus to rally the Syrian troops.

Marches a Syrian army to confront Ali at Siffin on the upper Euphrates in northern Syria. After weeks of skirmishing and a major battle in which his side is on the verge of defeat, his troops raise pages of the Quran on the points of their lances, calling for arbitration. Ali is forced by his own men to accept.

Arbitration is held at Dumat al-Jandal (or Adhruh according to other sources). The result is unclear in the historical record but is generally remembered as politically advantageous to Mu'awiya, leaving Ali's caliphate weakened. The Kharijite secession from Ali's army follows.

Has himself proclaimed caliph in Jerusalem (according to some sources) or in Damascus by the Syrian troops, while Ali is still alive. Sends raiding expeditions into Egypt and the Hijaz to weaken Ali's position.

After Ali's assassination in January 661 and the abdication of his son Hasan, marches to Kufa and is acknowledged as caliph by all the Muslim provinces. The year 41 AH is later remembered as the Year of the Community ('Am al-Jama'a) for ending the first civil war. He returns to Damascus, which he makes the permanent capital of the new Umayyad caliphate.

From his capital at Damascus, governs the Islamic empire from the Atlas mountains to the borders of India. Expands the bureaucracy, builds the first systematic naval power of the caliphate, and continues annual raids into Byzantine Anatolia. Establishes diwans (registries) for taxation and for the army payroll.

Around 670, launches a sustained naval and land campaign against the Byzantine capital Constantinople. The siege drags on (with annual raids) for several years and is famously commemorated by the death of the elderly companion Abu Ayyub al-Ansari outside the walls. The siege is eventually unsuccessful.

In 676 begins to extract pledges of allegiance from across the empire for his son Yazid as his successor, contrary to the precedent of consultation among the senior companions. This nomination is opposed by Husayn ibn Ali, Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr, Abd Allah ibn Umar, and others, but Mu'awiya pushes it through.

Dies in Damascus on 15 Rajab 60 AH (22 April 680), after a reign of nearly twenty years. He is buried in Damascus. His son Yazid succeeds him without significant opposition from within Syria, but his nomination triggers within months the second civil war, the killing of Husayn at Karbala, and the long-term Sunni-Shi'i split.
